One Olympian alone moves freely among the heavens, the earth, and the land of the dead, escorting souls to the river of the underworld. Name him.

Powerful and feared, this Olympian was nonetheless the most hated of all the gods — even Zeus told him so to his face. A rival deity stood for the disciplined, strategic side of the same domain. Name the hated god.
Dipped as a baby in a magic river, this warrior was unharmable everywhere except the one spot his mother gripped. He died from a single arrow there. Name him.
Winter comes, the Greeks said, from a goddess's grief: her daughter must spend part of each year below because she ate a few pomegranate seeds. Name the grieving mother, not the daughter.
Gods are deathless, yet one ancient story reports a god's death announced across the sea to a passing sailor. This horned god of the wild is the one the tale names as dead. Name him.
A hundred-eyed watchman was set to guard a girl Zeus loved; after Hermes killed him, a goddess saved his many eyes by setting them in the tail of her sacred bird. Name the watchman.
To kill a monster whose direct gaze turned men to stone, this hero never looked at her — he watched her reflection in a polished shield and struck. Name the hero.
One Norse god let a monstrous wolf bite off his hand so the other gods could chain it up. Odin is famous for losing an eye — this was a different god losing a different part. Who lost the hand?

dimension: a god who became a mother
q: Odin's eight-legged horse Sleipnir had an unusual mother: a Norse god who turned into a mare and gave birth to it. Which god was the mother?
To seal the truce ending their war, the Norse gods all spat into a vat, and from that spittle they made a being so wise he could answer any question. Who was made from the gods' spit?
At Ragnarok, the wolf Fenrir swallows Odin. One of Odin's other sons then avenges him by killing Fenrir and lives on into the renewed world. Which son?
Frigg made every thing in the world swear never to harm her son Baldr, but skipped one plant she thought too young to bother. Loki used that plant to kill Baldr. What plant was it?

dimension: the holdout who doomed a rescue
q: Hel agreed to release the dead Baldr if every single thing in the world wept for him. Everything wept except one giantess who refused, dooming him. By what name did this holdout go?

dimension: villain who was once a defender
q: We remember one Egyptian god as the murderer of Osiris and lord of chaos, but in older myth he stood at the prow of the sun god's boat each night, spearing the serpent that tried to stop the sun. Which god?

dimension: ruler vs embalmer of the dead
q: In Egyptian myth the jackal-headed god handles mummies and guides souls, but he is NOT the ruler of the afterlife. Which green-skinned god actually rules the realm of the dead and judges them?
At the weighing of the heart, one god runs the scales and a monster eats the failures, but a different ibis-headed god stands by to write down the verdict. Who is the record-keeper?

dimension: trick that beat the sun god
q: An Egyptian deity made a snake from Ra's own spit, let it bite him, then offered a cure only if Ra revealed his secret name — gaining power over him. Which deity pulled off this trick?

Ra sent a fierce lioness goddess to punish humans, but she went on such a killing spree that the gods flooded the fields with beer dyed red like blood to get her drunk and stop her. Which goddess nearly wiped out humanity?

Many myths tell of someone going to the land of the dead to retrieve a loved one and failing. In the JAPANESE version, a god goes to Yomi for his dead wife, breaks a rule by looking at her rotting body, and flees as she chases him. Which god?
In the AZTEC creation, a feathered-serpent god descends to the land of the dead to gather the bones of past humans, then sounds a hole-less conch to win them. Which god rebuilt humanity from bones?

In a MESOPOTAMIAN myth, a goddess of love and war descends to her sister's underworld; at each of seven gates she must give up one piece of clothing, arriving naked and powerless to be struck dead. Which goddess made this descent?

dimension: first to die rules the dead
q: In the HINDU Vedas, the lord of the dead earned his throne in an unusual way: he was the very first mortal ever to die, and so became the one who rules over all who follow. Which god?
A demon won a boon: he couldn't be killed by man or beast, indoors or out, by day or night, on ground or in air. Vishnu took a form that slipped every clause at once. Which avatar killed him?

dimension: how a god got an animal head
q: One Hindu god guards his mother's door, is beheaded by his own father for blocking him, and is then revived with the head of the first creature found — an elephant. Which god got his head this way?

dimension: the sun hides in a cave
q: In JAPANESE myth, the sun deity is offended by her storm-god brother and hides in a cave, plunging the world into darkness; the gods lure her out with a raucous dance and trick her with a mirror. Which sun deity hid?

Long before Noah, a MESOPOTAMIAN man survived a great flood in a boat, released birds to find land, and was then granted eternal life — later visited by Gilgamesh seeking that same gift. Who was this flood survivor?

An Irish god talked his way into the gods' hall not by one great skill but by claiming mastery of every art at once — and when no single god there could match all of them, the doors opened. Which god earned the title 'Master of All Arts'?
Egypt has its jackal-headed guide of the dead, but the AZTECs had a dog-headed god — twin of the feathered serpent — who guided souls through the underworld. Which god?
After Baldr died, one of Odin's sons borrowed the eight-legged horse Sleipnir and rode nine nights down to Hel to try to ransom him. Which god made the ride?
One Norse god was said to be born of nine mothers, need less sleep than a bird, and hear wool growing on a sheep's back. He guards the rainbow bridge into Asgard. Who is he?
A working machine for sending a copied image over a wire was first patented in roughly which decade — the same kind of device we'd later call a fax?
The first machine that gave you a product when you dropped in a coin was built in roughly which era — for dispensing holy water in a temple?
The digits we call 'Arabic numerals' were actually invented in which country?
The world's first true paper money — printed notes a government would honor — was issued in which country?
In which country did the inventor of the modern ballpoint pen file his successful patent after fleeing Europe — a country where the pen still bears his name?
The decimal point in written numbers first shows up in which century — it predates the invention of calculus?
The first working programmable, fully automatic digital computer (the Z3, 1941) was built in which country?
The windshield wiper was patented in roughly which decade — so early that the Ford Model T hadn't even been built yet?
The world's first SMS text message ('Merry Christmas') was sent in roughly which decade?
The oldest surviving photograph, a rooftop view that took many hours to expose, was made in roughly which decade?
The first commercial electric telegraph went into service in roughly which decade — well before the telephone existed?
Sealed canned food was patented in roughly which decade — so early that people opened the cans with a hammer and chisel for decades?
The first algorithm written to be run by a machine (for Babbage's never-built engine) was published in roughly which decade?
Gunpowder was first formulated in which country — by alchemists who were actually trying to brew an elixir of immortality?
The first true battery — a stack of metal discs giving a steady current — was built in roughly which decade?
The first practical single-rotor helicopter (the VS-300) first flew in which country in 1939?
The modern three-point seatbelt was invented by a carmaker in which country, which then made the patent free for everyone?
The Antikythera mechanism, the world's first known analog computer, was built by which ancient civilization (now a country)?
George Devol filed the patent for the first programmable industrial robot arm (Unimate) in roughly which decade?
The first self-contained digital camera (built at Kodak) was invented in roughly which decade?
The world's first webcam, set up to watch a coffee pot, was built at a university in which country?
So-called 'Panama hats' are actually woven in which country?
An interactive 'Tennis for Two' game, displayed on an oscilloscope for visitors, was built in roughly which decade — before Pong?
A loom that wove patterns from interchangeable punch cards — an early programmable machine — was demonstrated in roughly which decade?
The first networked email — introducing the @ in addresses — was sent in roughly which decade?
The first working laser, built from a ruby rod, was demonstrated in roughly which decade?
Among the world's ten most populous countries, one has never had a single citizen win a Nobel Prize. Which country?
Averaged over many years, which planet spends more time closer to Earth than any other?
Which planet has the hottest surface in the solar system, even though it is not the closest to the Sun?
How many bones does a typical adult human skeleton have?
How many bones are in a single human hand and wrist?
In which part of the body is the smallest bone in the human skeleton found?

dimension: most abundant element in the body
q: Which chemical element makes up the largest share of the human body by mass?
How many basic tastes does the human tongue detect, now that scientists include the savory one?
As of recent counts, which planet has the most confirmed moons?
About how many minutes does sunlight take to travel from the Sun to Earth?
Which country launched the first artificial satellite to orbit Earth?
Which country first put a human-made object onto the Moon's surface?
In which country was the world's first human-to-human heart transplant performed?
Which country sent the first spacecraft to fly past Pluto?
In which country was Dolly the sheep, the first mammal cloned from an adult cell, created?
In which country was the world's first IVF ('test-tube') baby born?
In which country was the first widely used periodic table of the elements created?
In which country were X-rays discovered in the 1890s?
In which decade was the Human Genome Project's sequence first completed?
In which decade was the metric system first established, during a famous revolution?
In which country was the first vaccine, against smallpox, developed in the 1790s?
Almost every country flies a four-sided flag. One country's flag is not four-sided at all — it's two stacked triangular pennants. Which country?
It is the only national flag that shows a modern assault rifle — an AK-47 with a bayonet — crossed over a hoe on an open book. Which country?
Two men — one holding an axe, one a paddle — stand on either side of the coat of arms at the center of this flag, making it the only flag with people as a main feature. Which country?
Only two national flags contain any of this color — one hides it in a parrot's feathers, the other in a rainbow. Historically it was the rarest, costliest dye. Which color?
Its flag shows the whole island's copper-colored outline above two olive branches — one of only two flags that put the country's own map front and center. Which country?
This is the only national flag whose front and back show different emblems — one side bears the national coat of arms, the other the treasury seal. Which country?
People often guess Brazil for the flag with the most stars, but Brazil's 27 lose to another country's 50. Which country actually has the most stars?
The stars on this country's flag are not decorative — they reproduce the exact night sky over its old capital at the moment it became a republic. Which country?
A dragon appears on only two well-known flags. The sovereign UN country flies a WHITE dragon clutching jewels across a diagonal yellow-and-orange field. Which country?
A few flags show a building, but only this one depicts a famous temple complex — a white silhouette of it sits on the red center band. Which country?
Almost every flag is wider than it is tall, but only this country's flag is more than twice as wide as it is high — an 11-by-28 stretch with a serrated white band. Which country?
With a Y-shaped split design, this is the only national flag built from six colors and no seal or emblem. Which country?
Its flag is a single off-center golden disc on a plain blue field — but unlike the look-alike sun flags, this disc is a full moon, prized for fishing and planting. Which country?
Because its green flag carries a sacred declaration of faith above a sword, this country never lowers its flag to half-mast, even in mourning. Which country?
A single green tree sits dead-center on this red-white-red flag, chosen for its biblical fame and longevity. Which country?
A two-headed eagle appears on several Balkan emblems, but only this country flies a black double-headed eagle alone on a plain red field as its whole flag. Which country?
A red-and-black flag with the Southern Cross and a golden bird-of-paradise was designed by a 15-year-old in a national contest. Which country?
Its flag shows a golden lion gripping a sword, framed by four sacred fig leaves in the corners. Which country?
Its central emblem shows an eagle perched on a cactus devouring a snake — the exact omen a people were told to seek when founding their capital city. Which country?
Three countries fly plain red-and-white two-stripe flags so alike they cause mix-ups — but only one puts WHITE on top of RED. Which country?
A white off-center cross on red, this is recognized as the oldest national flag still in continuous use — and the template every other Nordic flag copied. Which country?
Its flag shows a golden frigatebird soaring over a rising sun, above three wavy white stripes for the ocean and its three island groups. Which country?
Only two countries fly a square flag. The one that is NOT the Vatican shows a bold white cross on red. Which country?
On this green flag the off-center red disc stands for the blood of those lost in a liberation war, and the green for fertile land — not for Islam. Which country?
Its red flag with a white crescent and star comes from an empire that adopted the symbol after conquering Constantinople — and is why many later read the crescent-and-star as Islamic. Which country?
A green parrot ringed by ten green stars sits on a cross-divided field of green, yellow, and black on this Caribbean island's flag. Which country?
Its flag centers a single stylized leaf between two red bars — and despite looking ornate, that leaf has exactly eleven points. Which country?
The other square flag besides Switzerland splits into yellow and white halves with crossed keys and a papal tiara. Which sovereign state?
A tiny European principality has a red-over-white flag nearly identical to a huge Southeast Asian nation's. Which is the small European one?
A Central African country has blue-yellow-red vertical stripes almost indistinguishable from a European country's flag. Which is the African one?
Which country brought back its pre-1969 red-black-green crescent flag in 2011 after an uprising toppled a long-ruling leader?
Which African country, founded by freed American slaves, has a flag of red and white stripes with a single white star in a blue field?
Which country's flag is officially turned upside down, with the red stripe on top, to signal that the nation is at war?
Which country has the most recently changed national flag, after it added red stripes above and below its green field in 2017?
Which Caribbean country's flag, a gold diagonal cross over green and black, uses none of the three most common flag colors — red, white, or blue?
Which African country changed its flag to a full sun in 2010, then reverted to its original rising-sun flag just two years later?
Which part of the United Kingdom, despite its famous red dragon, has no symbol represented in the Union Jack?
One powder-based process needs no support structures at all, because the loose powder around each layer holds the part up while it builds. Which process is it?

dimension: how a layer is exposed to light
q: Two resin processes both harden liquid with light. One uses a projector to flash a whole layer at once instead of a moving laser dot. Which one flashes the full layer?
On most desktop resin printers the part is built hanging upside down and is slowly lifted up out of a shallow tank of liquid. What is this build setup called?
One common filament shrinks so much as it cools that it can warp or crack unless the printer is boxed in to hold the heat. Which plastic is it?
Most printers make a part in a single color. Which powder-and-glue process can lay down full color across the whole part by mixing colored binders as it prints?
On a two-nozzle FDM printer, one special material is printed as the support and then simply rinsed away in water afterward. Which material is it?
Which filament is prized as a middle ground: stronger and more heat- and chemical-resistant than the easy beginner plastic, yet far less prone to warping than the one that needs an enclosure?
One inkjet-style process sprays droplets of liquid resin and cures them on the fly, so a single part can blend several colors and even soft and hard regions at once. Which process is it?
International standards sort every additive process into a fixed number of big families, like material extrusion and powder bed fusion. How many families are there?

dimension: first-layer helper that does not touch the part
q: On an FDM print, a loop of filament is drawn around the part before it starts, but never touches the part. What is that loop called?
One metal process is used less to make whole new parts and more to repair worn ones, by melting metal as it is sprayed onto an existing component. Which process is it?
Which beginner-friendly filament prints at low heat with no heated bed needed, but will sag and deform if the part is left in a hot car?
One whole family of additive machines doesn't melt or cure anything. Instead it stacks sheets of material, glues them down, and cuts each layer to shape. What is this family called?
In a slicer you set how solid the inside of a part is. One popular pattern is a wavy, three-dimensional lattice that is strong in every direction. What is that pattern called?

dimension: fully melting vs partially fusing metal powder
q: Two metal powder-bed processes are often confused. One fully melts the metal into a near-solid pool rather than just heating it enough to stick together. Which one fully melts it?

A Japanese researcher built a working machine that hardened liquid plastic with UV light to grow shapes layer by layer. People assume the technology was born in Silicon Valley in the 1980s. Which decade did he first demonstrate it?
The very first stereolithography patent that launched the industry was filed by an American working at a company that made UV coatings. In which decade was that patent granted?

answer_subject: A university (University of Texas at Austin)
q: The man who built the first laser-sintering machine, which fuses powder into solid parts, did it as a student project. At which kind of institution was that pioneering work done?
One company runs what is widely called the world's largest 3D printing operation, churning out over a million unique parts every day. What everyday product does it actually make?
One medical device has quietly been built almost entirely by 3D printing for roughly two decades, with nearly the whole industry switching over. Which custom-fit device is it?
A famous jet-engine part was redesigned so that around 18 separately made pieces became a single printed piece. What was the new part count for that component?
A widely cited 'world's first' had an 83-year-old woman receive a complete printed titanium lower jaw. Which country's researchers and manufacturer made it?
The open-source project that aimed to build a 3D printer able to copy its own parts kicked off the cheap-printer boom. In which decade did it begin?

The company that built the first commercial 3D printer and the company that pioneered the melted-plastic FDM process became the two giants of the field. Both were founded in the same decade. Which one?
The first object ever 3D printed in space was made on the International Space Station. What broad material category was that first space-made part?
When the industry finally agreed on the formal umbrella name 'additive manufacturing' and set up a standards committee for it, in which decade did that happen?
The first 3D-printed firearm to actually fire, the Liberator, made global headlines. In which decade was it built and test-fired?
The fuel nozzle that made GE famous in 3D printing is built by fusing metal powder with a laser. Which broad process family does that belong to?

Early desktop 3D printers suddenly became affordable around 2009 largely because a key invention's legal protection ran out. Roughly how many years does a US patent's protection last, which sets that clock?
Twelve of the thirteen colonies sent delegates to the First Continental Congress in 1774. Which one sent none at all?
Which of the thirteen states refused to send any delegate at all to the 1787 Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia?
The Declaration of Independence and the Constitution feel like one founding cast, but they were signed eleven years apart. How many men signed both documents?
On the final day of the Constitutional Convention, how many delegates who were still present in the room refused to sign the document?
The old Articles of Confederation required all states to agree. Under the Constitution itself, how many state ratifications were needed before it would take effect?
We remember the Bill of Rights as ten amendments, but Congress actually sent more than that to the states in 1789. How many amendments did Congress send?
Fifty-five delegates attended the Constitutional Convention, but most were gone or refused by the end. How many signatures were actually on the Constitution when the convention closed?
James Madison is called the 'Father of the Constitution,' but a different writer penned more than half of the 85 Federalist essays nearly single-handedly. Who wrote the largest share?
At just 26 years old, who was the youngest man to sign the Declaration of Independence?
Colonial religious refuge usually brings the Puritans to mind, but which Chesapeake colony was founded as a haven for persecuted English Catholics?
Three small river towns adopted a written frame of government based on the people's consent, often called the first written constitution in the colonies. In which colony?
People assume Massachusetts or Virginia led the way, but which colony was the first to formally authorize its delegates to vote for independence, nearly three months before the July vote?
Washington was inaugurated and the first government met under the new Constitution in which city, not Philadelphia and not the capital that did not yet exist?
The Revolution is bracketed by 1776 and the 1783 peace treaty, but in what year did the decisive battle at Yorktown, where Cornwallis surrendered, take place?
People place the first national government alongside the 1776 Declaration, but in which decade did the Articles of Confederation finally take effect after years of ratification delays?
People cluster colonial unrest right before independence, but in which decade did Parliament pass the Stamp Act that sparked 'no taxation without representation'?
The Central Pacific started in Sacramento and the Union Pacific near Omaha, but in which state did the two halves of the first transcontinental railroad actually meet at the golden spike?
The Union Pacific laid its first transcontinental track westward from Omaha. In which state did that eastern construction terminus sit, given Council Bluffs across the river is in Iowa?
Pickett's Charge was led by Virginians under a Virginia general, but in which state did that failed assault, the 'high-water mark of the Confederacy,' actually take place?
Grant's siege that split the Confederacy and won the Union full control of the Mississippi River ended at Vicksburg. In which state is Vicksburg?
Many guess Virginia or the capital area, but in which state, the first to secede, did Confederate guns fire on Fort Sumter to open the Civil War?
Custer's famous fight pulls people toward Montana, but in which state, on the Pine Ridge reservation, did the Wounded Knee Massacre, the last major clash of the Indian Wars, occur?
'Custer's Last Stand' feels like Dakota or Wyoming country to many, but in which state did the Battle of the Little Bighorn actually take place?
People expect a progressive Northeastern state, but which frontier state was the first where women could vote, granting them full voting rights as a territory decades before the 19th Amendment?
The Trail of Tears is remembered by where it started in Georgia and the Southeast, but the Cherokee were forced west to Indian Territory in what present-day state?
The crowds of 'forty-niners' arrived in 1849, but the gold strike at Sutter's Mill that set off the Gold Rush happened the year before, in which state?
People link the Erie Canal to opening the Midwest and reach for an Ohio or Great Lakes state, but the entire 363-mile canal lies within which single state?
The huge Mexican Cession feels like it belongs alongside the 1850s slavery compromises, but in which decade was the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, ending the Mexican-American War, signed?
Alaska feels like a modern frontier, so people guess late, but in which decade, just after the Civil War, did the U.S. buy Alaska from Russia in the deal mocked as 'Seward's Folly'?
People tie the Statue of Liberty to the 1876 centennial that inspired it or to the 1890s immigration peak, but in which decade was the statue itself dedicated in New York Harbor?
This legendary symbol of the West feels like it lasted years, but how long did the Pony Express actually operate before the telegraph made it obsolete?
People remember the Homestead Act offered 'free land' but forget the size of a standard claim. How many acres did a settler get for farming and living on it for five years?
The Missouri Compromise is remembered for pairing Missouri and Maine, but its real point was the resulting Senate balance. How many free states and how many slave states did the Union then hold?
People know a cluster of Western states entered the Union together around 1889–1890 but rarely the count. How many states were admitted in that burst?
The last land the U.S. bought from Mexico sounds like it should touch California or Texas, but the Gadsden Purchase actually rounded out which two of today's states?
People remember a 'line' divided slave and free territory under the Missouri Compromise but not where it ran. It was not the Mason-Dixon line, so which parallel was it?
After a wartime Supreme Court ruling let states set one voting age for federal elections and another for their own, which amendment, ratified in about 100 days, fixed that split nationwide?
It was written by James Madison and proposed alongside the Bill of Rights, yet a college student's term paper revived it and it became law more than 200 years later. Which amendment is it?
Congress proposes amendments with two-thirds, but ratification takes three-fourths of the states. With 50 states today, how many must say yes for an amendment to become law?
Look at every U.S. paper bill in circulation. People assume only presidents appear on money, but how many of those faces never served as president?
The Supreme Court first struck down an act of Congress as unconstitutional, inventing judicial review. People often guess the 1790s, but in which ten-year span did that ruling actually fall?
A unanimous Supreme Court ruled segregated public schools unconstitutional, overturning 'separate but equal.' Many tie this to the marches and guess too late, so name the decade.
Its nickname is a fact about the Constitution: by a unanimous convention vote in December 1787, which state was the very first to ratify it?
People file the Wall Street crash that kicked off the Great Depression with the 1930s, but in which decade did Black Tuesday actually fall?
Several western states let women vote first, but which state cast the 36th and deciding vote to ratify the 19th Amendment, when a young legislator changed his mind after a note from his mother?
People link Rosa Parks loosely to Selma or Birmingham, but in which Alabama city did the 1955-56 bus boycott sparked by her arrest actually take place?
It's easy to anchor on the Apollo 11 crew of three, but how many test pilots made up NASA's first group of astronauts, announced in 1959?
We reflexively say 50 states, but when Arizona joined in 1912 and completed the contiguous map, how many states were in the Union?
It's the worst pandemic most people can name before COVID, so many misplace it later, but in which decade did the influenza pandemic that killed about 675,000 Americans strike?
People bundle civil-rights landmarks into the 1960s, but in which decade did the Supreme Court rule school segregation unconstitutional in Brown v. Board of Education?
The Soviet Union dissolved in 1991, so people round the Wall's fall up, but in which decade did the Berlin Wall actually fall?
Many assume so sweeping a program came in the postwar boom or the Great Society, but in which decade was the Social Security Act, creating old-age insurance, signed?
People focus on the speaker and forget the setting, or confuse it with Selma or Atlanta, but in which city was the 'I Have a Dream' speech delivered at the 1963 march?
Relative to the mother's own body weight, which bird lays the largest egg?
